lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Or close the wall up with our English dead.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: In peace there’s nothing so becomes a man

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As modest stillness and humility:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But when the blast of war blows in our ears,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then imitate the action of the tiger;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Stiffen the sinews, summon up the blood,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Disguise fair nature with hard-favour’d rage;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then lend the eye a terrible aspect;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Let it pry through the portage of the head

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Like the brass cannon; let the brow o’erwhelm it

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As fearfully as doth a galled rock

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O’erhang and jutty his confounded base,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Swill’d with the wild and wasteful ocean.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now set the teeth and stretch the nostril wide,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Hold hard the breath and bend up every spirit

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To his full height. On, on, you noblest English,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whose blood is fet from fathers of war-proof!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Fathers that, like so many Alexanders,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Have in these parts from morn till even fought

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And sheathed their swords for lack of argument:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Dishonour not your mothers; now attest

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That those whom you call’d fathers did beget you.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Be copy now to men of grosser blood,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And teach them how to war. And you, good yeomen,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whose limbs were made in England, show us here

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The mettle of your pasture; let us swear

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That you are worth your breeding; which I doubt not;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For there is none of you so mean and base,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That hath not noble lustre in your eyes.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I see you stand like greyhounds in the slips,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Straining upon the start. The game’s afoot:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Follow your spirit, and upon this charge

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Cry, “God for Harry, England, and Saint George!”

stage_directions.text: Alarum. Enter King Henry, Exeter, Bedford, Gloucester and Soldiers, with scaling-ladders.
Exeunt. Alarum, and chambers go off.

glosses.definition: once again into the gap in the walls — soldiers must renew the assault through the opening a siege-cannon has torn

glosses.phrase: close the wall up with our English dead
glosses.definition: fill the breach with our corpses — either break through or die in the gap
quiet restraint — Henry's theory of peacetime virtue
trumpet-call of war; the din of battle

glosses.phrase: imitate the action of the tiger
glosses.definition: become predatory and ferocious — the speech's keynote
harden the muscles and tendons

glosses.phrase: Disguise fair nature with hard-favour’d rage
glosses.definition: mask your gentle face with ugly fury
fearsome expression
the skull's openings (eye-sockets) treated like gun-ports in a ship
the eye as an artillery piece — warlike metamorphosis of the body
a cliff weathered by the sea
project over its eroded footing — 'jutty' = overhang

glosses.phrase: Swill’d with the wild and wasteful ocean
glosses.definition: drenched by the destructive sea
drawn from battle-tested fathers — 'fet' = fetched; 'war-proof' = proven in war
Alexander the Great — Macedonian conqueror; the play's first of three invocations
from morning until evening

glosses.phrase: sheathed their swords for lack of argument
glosses.definition: put their swords away for want of an opponent to fight

glosses.phrase: those whom you call’d fathers did beget you
glosses.definition: the men you call father really did father you — prove your paternity in battle

glosses.phrase: copy now to men of grosser blood
glosses.definition: a model for coarser-blooded men
free-born English commoners of the farming class — the backbone of the English army and longbow
the quality of your home soil — show what English ground produces
humble and low-born
the shine of nobility in your eyes — social elevation via valour
greyhounds in their leashes — hunting dogs straining at the start
pulling against the leash at the starting line
the quarry is up and running — hunting idiom, later made famous by Sherlock Holmes

glosses.phrase: God for Harry, England, and Saint George
glosses.definition: the English war-cry — St. George is England's patron saint and the Garter's namesake

annotations.title: The rhetorical architecture of 'Once more unto the breach'
annotations.body: Henry's speech to the soldiers at the Harfleur breach is a masterclass in siege oratory. Its structure is rigorously controlled. Lines 1–2 give the imperative and its stakes (return to the breach, or die filling it). Lines 3–5 mark the ethical pivot: what virtue is in peace is not what virtue is in war. Lines 6–14 supply the key metamorphic command: imitate the tiger, stiffen sinews, summon blood, disguise fair nature with hard-favour'd rage. The body-language is physical: teeth set, nostrils stretched, breath held, every spirit bent up. The rhetorical technique is transformation — Henry is not asking the soldiers to be braver; he is prescribing a bodily and facial reconfiguration that will make them killers. The speech is also addressed in two registers: first to the noblest ('you noblest English / Whose blood is fet from fathers of war-proof'), then to the yeomen ('good yeomen / Whose limbs were made in England'), closing with the single collective war-cry. The rhetorical care is near-liturgical.
sources: Brian Vickers, The Artistry of Shakespeare's Prose (1968)

annotations.title: Class-addressed oratory — the yeomen and the game
annotations.body: Henry's speech explicitly addresses the yeomen — the free-born English farming commoners who made up the archer corps — and praises 'the mettle of your pasture.' This acknowledges a military reality: the longbow, which would decide Agincourt, was the yeoman's weapon, not the knight's. The speech flatters the social group that would actually win the battle. The closing image — 'I see you stand like greyhounds in the slips, / Straining upon the start. The game's afoot' — converts the army into a hunting pack and the French into quarry. 'The game's afoot' has become proverbial (and famously re-used by Arthur Conan Doyle's Sherlock Holmes). In context, it is the speech's decisive detheologizing move: what began as a siege-ethic ends as a blood-sport. The breach-ethic is not 'kill in the name of God' but 'chase the prey.' The closing invocation 'God for Harry, England, and Saint George' nominally rebalances this, but the hunt-image lingers.
sources: Juliet Barker, Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle (2005)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

trials.prompt: What does Henry command the soldiers to do in lines 6–14?
options: Undergo a bodily and facial metamorphosis — imitate the tiger, stiffen sinews, disguise fair nature with 'hard-favour'd rage' — a physical re-engineering into killers

trials.prompt: Why does Henry explicitly address the yeomen?
options: They were the lowest social class present
Because the yeoman archers — commoners with longbows — were the army's decisive force; the speech flatters the social group that will actually win the battle
Because the nobility had refused to fight

trials.prompt: What is the structural function of 'I see you stand like greyhounds in the slips' (line 31)?
options: A reference to Henry's hunting hobby
A closing simile that converts the army into a hunting pack and the French into quarry — 'The game's afoot' (line 32) makes the siege a blood-sport

trials.prompt: The speech's final line invokes which three names?
options: God, Harry, England, and Saint George (the English patron saint)
Saint Denis, Saint George, and the Virgin
